description:
# Why

`bl link add A <type> B` rejects B with `error: task not found` when B
is closed. The lookup goes through the live store at state-branch HEAD,
where closed tasks have been archived; `bl show B` still finds it via
history reconstruction, but `bl link add` doesn't.

This shows up consistently when filing follow-up balls that want to
point back at the just-shipped parent. Today's session hit it three
times in a row trying to `relates_to` bl-38dd (closed minutes earlier)
and bl-ffb4. The documented workaround
([[feedback_link_add_closed]]) is to put the canonical id in the close
--note or in the new ball's description prose. Both work, but they
defeat the discoverability promise of links — `bl show <closed-id>`
won't surface the new ball as a reverse link, because the link was
never recorded.

# Concrete shape

Either:

- **Soft path.** `bl link add` falls back to the same history scan
  `bl show` uses when the live store lookup misses. Link is added to
  the *open* side only (A's record); the closed side has nothing
  mutable to write to, but a reader of B's archived record could
  surface inbound links by walking the live store at scan time. That
  matches the existing read-side asymmetry — `bl show` already
  reconstructs closed tasks for display.

- **Stricter path.** Allow it but require a flag
  (`--allow-closed-target`) so the operator confirms they understand
  the asymmetry. Avoids accidental link-rot from auto-completion on a
  stale id.

The soft path matches the principle that links are non-blocking
metadata for everything except `gates` (which already blocks close, so
linking-to-closed there is incoherent and should stay rejected). Other
types — `relates_to`, `duplicates`, `supersedes`, `replies_to` — have
no reason to care about the target's lifecycle.

# Out of scope

- Reverse-link materialization on the closed side (would require
  rewriting an archived task or maintaining a sidecar index — bigger
  question, not blocking this).
- `gates` semantics — stay rejected; a closed gate target is a no-op
  blocker.

# Origin

bl-38dd / bl-773e / bl-eb76 session — three retrospective cross-refs
in a row blocked by the closed-target rule, all genuinely useful
links, all worked around with prose references. Lossy on
discoverability.
title: bl link add: accept closed targets for retrospective cross-refs
